title: Cannot set RT priority in container
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I tried to run a short program in a podman container. When I tried to set the priority to realtime, I always get EPERM as errno. I already tried to run podman with the flag --cpu-rt-runtime=950000. 

The program:
```
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sched.h>
#include <pthread.h>
#include <errno.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
  printf("Setting rt prio\n");

  struct sched_param param;
  param.sched_priority=20;
  int retval = sched_setscheduler(0, SCHED_RR, &param);
  printf("Retval: %d, Errno: %d\n", retval, errno);

  return 0;
 }
```


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Compile the following program with gcc -o realtime realtime.c -lpthread

2. podman run -it  --entrypoint=/bin/sh realtime

3. ./realtime

**Describe the results you received:**
/ # ./realtime
Setting rt prio
Retval: 1, Errno: 1

**Describe the results you expected:**
I would expect that this call should be ok.
/ # ./realtime
Setting rt prio
Retval: 0, Errno: 0

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
I am running on WSL2 under windows. When I tried to add the flag --cpu-rt-runtime=950000 I got the following error:

Tried the same with runc itself, there it worked.

DEBU[0000] ExitCode msg: "writing file `cpu.rt_runtime_us`: invalid argument: oci runtime error"
Error: OCI runtime error: writing file `cpu.rt_runtime_us`: Invalid argument

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.4.2
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


Yes

I am using WSL2, tried with Ubuntu 20.04 and Debian distro.

comments.body:
@mario19911 I think you would need more privileges or alteast `CAP_SYS_NICE` , could you try with `podman run --security-opt seccomp=unconfined --cap-add all -it --entrypoint=/bin/sh realtime`

>I am running on WSL2 under windows. When I tried to add the flag --cpu-rt-runtime=950000 I got the following error:
>Tried the same with runc itself, there it worked.
>DEBU[0000] ExitCode msg: "writing file cpu.rt_runtime_us: invalid argument: oci runtime error"
>Error: OCI runtime error: writing file cpu.rt_runtime_us: Invalid argument

Could you elaborate more here ? Do you mean `--cpu-rt-runtime=950000` is not processed by `crun` properly while it works with `runc` ?

@mario19911 For the `invalid argument` it looks more like that there is less CPU bandwidth available than requested. I'd suggest testing with a smaller value with `podman/crun` `--cpu-rt-runtime=95000`.

@flouthoc : Thanks for the fast answer.
I just ran it with ``podman run -it --mount type=bind,source=realtime,target=/realtime --cap-add all --security-opt seccomp=unconfined --entrypoint=/bin/sh rtus/rtum85/04.mj``. This also failed for me.

Additionally I tried to add the ``--cpu-rt-runtime=950000`` and again got the error ``Error: OCI runtime error: writing file `cpu.rt_runtime_us`: Invalid argument``. Also with the smaller value of 95000 I got the same error. It only works when I set it to 0.

Regarding runc: I tried the same with podman + runc --> same result. When I set up a config.json and started the same with runc only, it worked.

> Regarding runc: I tried the same with podman + runc --> same result. When I set up a config.json and started the same with runc only, it worked.

does the same config.json file work with crun?

Could you share the config.json file you've used?

As you suggested I also tested it with crun and the same config.json --> this worked perfectly fine!

``
root@md3bex3c:/home# crun run s8000
/ # ./realtime
Setting rt prio
Retval: 0, Errno: 0
``

What might be of interest:

**The following I executed on my system:**
``root@md3bex3c:/home# cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.rt_runtime_us`` --> 950000

**In the crun container:**
``root@md3bex3c:/home# cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.rt_runtime_us`` --> 950000

**In the podman container:**
``root@md3bex3c:/home# cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/cpu.rt_runtime_us`` --> 0

@mario19911 One difference is your custom config with `crun/runc` also sets `realtimePeriod` while with podman you aren't doing that could you try with `--cpu-rt-runtime=950000` and `--cpu-rt-period=1000000` .

I tried the following two calls:
**First: just setting the period:**
``podman run -it  --cap-add all --security-opt seccomp=unconfined --cpu-rt-period=1000000 --entrypoint=/bin/sh realtime`` --> here I got the same issue when executing the realtime program.

**Second: setting period and runtime:**
``podman run -it --cap-add all --security-opt seccomp=unconfined --cpu-rt-period=1000000 --cpu-rt-runtime=950000 --entrypoint=/bin/sh realtime``

Here I get the error ``Error: OCI runtime error: writing file `cpu.rt_runtime_us`: Invalid argument``

one difference when you run the container manually is that it is not setting a new group.

Since you are using the cgroupfs, please make sure the parent cgroup has the correct settings, you may need to manually do something like:

```
# echo 950000 > /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/libpod_parent/cpu.rt_runtime_us
# echo 1000000 > /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/libpod_parent/cpu.rt_period_us
```

or specify a different `--parent-cgroup`

Thank you very much, with this settings it worked :) 

Just for my understanding - when I use systemd as cgroup manager do I have to do the same?

> Just for my understanding - when I use systemd as cgroup manager do I have to do the same?

yes, systemd doesn't manage the realtime CPU controller.
